completeness.rationale: All 7 expected information items are present in the triage summary: memory growth from ~500MB to 4GB+, v2.3 upgrade as trigger (~1 week ago), ~200 active users, consistent day-to-day pattern, daily restart as workaround, v2.3 changelog notification system mention, and 8GB RAM VM specs. The agent additionally captured weekend usage patterns, impact severity, reproduction steps, and proposed test cases.

accuracy.rationale: The triage correctly identifies v2.3 as the trigger and the real-time notifications system as the likely source, which satisfies the first acceptable diagnostic path. The mechanism is slightly off — the agent hypothesizes per-session/disconnect cleanup failure rather than the actual per-request listener registration without cleanup — but this is a reasonable interpretation of the same subsystem and matches acceptable paths. No significant inaccuracies that would mislead a developer.

thoroughness.rationale: Two substantive turns before resolving, with both yielding important information. The strong v2.3 + notification system lead from the reporter's first reply provided enough basis to resolve. However, the agent did not ask for server-side error logs or whether disabling notifications stops the leak — both noted as information gaps but left unasked. Given the strength of the lead, early resolution is defensible, but at least one of these diagnostic follow-ups would have been valuable.

economy.rationale: Both turns were productive. Turn 1's weekend usage question was particularly well-targeted. Turn 2's database and Node.js runtime questions were lower-value given the reporter had already noted the same setup worked on v2.2, but asking for OS and deployment topology was useful. At most one sub-question could have been dropped.

question_quality.rationale: The weekend/low-usage question in turn 1 was genuinely insightful — it confirmed usage-correlated growth and ruled out time-based leaks. The second turn's environment questions were professional and standard. Questions were well-framed and led to useful information the reporter might not have volunteered unprompted (the reporter volunteered the v2.3 lead themselves, but the weekend usage pattern was elicited by the agent's question).

actionability.rationale: The recommended fix section provides four specific, ordered investigation steps targeting the notification module's connection lifecycle. The heap snapshot suggestion, the v2.2-vs-v2.3 diff suggestion, and the proposed load test are concrete and immediately actionable. A developer could begin investigation and write a reproducer without any additional context.

notable_strengths: Captured all expected information items, including some the reporter only mentioned in passing
Correctly pinpointed v2.3 notification system as the root cause despite the reporter volunteering that information — the agent recognized and properly weighted it
Recommended fix section is unusually detailed and actionable, with specific investigation steps and a heap snapshot suggestion
Proposed test case is concrete and directly tied to the hypothesized failure mode
notable_weaknesses: Did not ask for server-side error logs or warnings, which often accompany listener accumulation (e.g., MaxListenersExceeded warnings)
Did not ask whether disabling the real-time notifications feature prevents the leak — a high-value diagnostic question given the strong hypothesis
Mechanism in root cause hypothesis is slightly off: hypothesizes per-session/disconnect failure rather than per-request listener registration without cleanup
most_insightful_question: Does the memory climb happen even during low-usage periods (e.g., weekends), or only when users are actively working?
missed_opportunities: Ask whether the server logs show any MaxListenersExceeded warnings or other errors correlated with memory growth
Ask whether disabling or opting out of real-time notifications (if possible) stops or reduces the memory climb — would confirm the hypothesis before resolving
Ask for the approximate number of page loads or API requests per user per hour to calculate the per-request leak rate and cross-check against observed memory growth
